119	### 5. cross-subject-eeg -- cross-paradigm cross-subject diagnostic on MOABB + FM probe
120	
121	**Background.** Run the five-part evaluation program from cross-subject-eeg.md section 6 under leakage-clean splits: (a) subject + dataset + hardware-disjoint splits simultaneously, (b) 0/1/5/20-shot calibration curves per subject, (c) per-subject performance distributions, (d) pre-training-overlap audit, (e) Riemannian baselines under same splits. Datasets: MOABB (14 MI datasets), EEG Foundation Challenge HBN Releases 1-11 (~2,600 subjects, CC-BY-SA-4.0, no DUA, confirmed in gap-closing pass).
122	
123	**Null result scenarios and their value.**
124	
125	Null A: Foundation models do NOT outperform Riemannian baselines under leakage-clean splits. This is arguably the most likely outcome given EEG-FM-Bench finding that compact architectures with domain-specific inductive biases consistently outperform significantly larger models. A null here IS the contribution: it quantifies the performance ceiling of large EEG-FMs for cross-subject transfer under honest conditions -- exactly what the EEG Foundation Models Critical Review (arXiv:2507.11783) calls for but does not deliver.
126	
127	Null B: Pre-training-overlap audit finds no overlap between evaluation sets and pre-training corpora. This null is also informative: it clears the evaluated FMs of data-contamination suspicion and makes their cross-subject numbers trustworthy.
128	
129	Null C: No bimodal per-subject distribution (BCI illiteracy rate lower than the 15-30% prior under modern FMs). This would update the field on whether the illiteracy problem persists under large pretrained models.
130	
131	**Power for per-subject distribution characterization.** Detecting BCI illiteracy rate 30% vs null baseline 10% at 80% power requires N=62 subjects. MOABB aggregated across datasets has hundreds of subjects; PhysionetMI alone has 109. Adequate for aggregate analysis. BCI-IV-2a (9 subjects) is trivially underpowered alone and must not be the sole dataset.
132	
133	**Power for leakage-inflated vs clean-split comparison.** If published leakage-inflated accuracy is ~72% and clean-split accuracy is ~58% (14pp gap per the EEG-FM-Bench finding pattern): N required = 182 subjects. MOABB aggregation clears this.
134	
135	**Pre-registration requirement.** The five-part evaluation program must be pre-registered before running any experiment: which FM checkpoints, which datasets, which split procedure, what constitutes overlap in the pre-training audit. Without pre-registration, the contribution risks being read as post-hoc cherry-picking of evaluation conditions.
136	
137	**Compute.** Riemannian baselines (pyRiemann): CPU-only, trivial. FM inference as frozen feature extractor: feasible within 4 GB for LaBraM / BENDR / BIOT. Full FM fine-tuning is out of scope and not needed for a diagnostic contribution.
138	
139	**Null risk symmetry.** Highly symmetric. FM wins, FM loses, overlap found, overlap absent, bimodal distribution confirmed or disconfirmed -- all are informative. The contribution is the diagnostic infrastructure and leakage-clean numbers, not a particular performance level. This is the most null-symmetric scope in the portfolio.
140	
141	**Verdict: defensible-null.** All plausible outcomes are informative. Power is adequate at MOABB aggregation scale. Pre-registration is the one non-negotiable condition.

145	### 6. affective-state -- feature-stability audit sub-scope (arXiv:2508.10561)
146	
147	**Background.** Replicate and extend the finding from arXiv:2508.10561 that only 2 of 164 EDA features show reproducible, statistically significant association with arousal across studies. The AHBU contribution: independent replication applied to cardiac features (HRV, ECG-derived) on a new combination of public datasets (DEAP, WESAD, MAHNOB-HCI).
148	
149	**Clarifying the unit of analysis.** This is a feature-level cross-dataset reproducibility study. N=164 features is the sample size for the binomial test. Confirming the 2/164 finding is statistically robust: P(X<=2 | n=164, p=0.05 expected base rate) = 0.0104; P(X<=2 | n=164, p=0.10) = 5.8e-06; P(X<=2 | n=164, p=0.20) = 1.1e-13. Even at a generous 5% expected base reproducibility rate, observing only 2/164 is significant at p=0.01. The finding is well-powered as a binomial test at N=164.
150	
151	**Null result scenario.** Headline null for AHBU = when cardiac features (HRV, ECG power-spectral features) are tested across DEAP/WESAD/MAHNOB on arousal, MORE than 2% show reproducibility. This null is informative: it establishes that cardiac features have higher cross-study stability than EDA, supporting the arousal-reframing angle and motivating a cardiac-arousal-decoding-done-honestly sub-scope.
152	
153	**Non-redundancy.** arXiv:2508.10561 focused on EDA features. Brookshire 2024 focused on DNN-EEG leakage. Apicella 2024 reviewed cross-subject methods. None tested cardiac feature reproducibility across studies in this way. The AHBU contribution is non-redundant if extended to cardiac features.
154	
155	**Conditions for informative null.**
156	(i) Feature list specified before running analysis (pre-registered). Reporting only the features that turn out reproducible post-hoc recapitulates the original paper trivially. The contribution requires testing a NEW feature set on NEW dataset combinations.
157	(ii) Datasets listed and justified before analysis. DEAP uses stimulus-class labels, not self-report; WESAD has strong within-session structure that can inflate reproducibility if sessions are not disjoint across studies. Both hazards must be documented.
158	(iii) Arousal operationalization consistent across datasets. DEAP uses 2-level arousal from stimulus rating; WESAD uses 3-class affect condition. Harmonization must be stated and justified before feature testing begins.
159	
160	**Compute.** Feature extraction from HRV/ECG on DEAP/WESAD/MAHNOB: CPU-only, trivial. Statistical testing with FDR correction: no GPU. The scope fits the 4 GB constraint with room to spare.
161	
162	**Null risk symmetry.** Symmetric. High cardiac reproducibility motivates the cardiac-arousal sub-scope. Low cardiac reproducibility confirms field-wide feature instability and motivates do-not-use-these-features-until-stability-is-established. Both outcomes are publishable.
163	
164	**Verdict: defensible-null.** Both directions informative, adequately powered as a feature-count binomial test, non-redundant with the EDA-focused source paper. Three conditions must be pre-registered: feature list, datasets, arousal operationalization. Failure to pre-register converts this to a near-null.

# Critic pass -- ecg-ppg-realworld methodology -- 2026-05-02

## Verdict

**pass-with-fixes**

The four artifacts are coherent, honest, and substantially well-designed. Pre-registration is genuinely complete and the held-out discipline is the strongest in the portfolio. Three issues require fixes before layer 30 proceeds: one major (PPV metric definition is ambiguous at coverage=0.80 -- does not specify how to handle non-AFIB records in the predicted subset), one major (P-4 pilot trains on strat_fold 1 only but then runs temperature scaling on strat_fold 9, which was reserved as the calibration partition -- this contaminates the calibration step if the pilot T value influences any design decision), and one minor (the cross-reference to ``cross-subject-eeg/approach.md §Promote`` in approach.md implies that artifact exists; it does not exist in the worktree as of this pass). No finding rises to block.

---

## Findings

### Major

**M-1 -- PPV metric definition ambiguous for the low-prevalence setting (approach.md §Evaluation protocol / protocol-lock.md §4)**

The protocol defines PPV(coverage=0.80) as TP / (TP + FP) among the top-80%-confidence records. But this definition conflates two distinct things: (a) the model's class-predicted label (AFIB vs not-AFIB), and (b) the model's confidence score used to select the top 80%. The formal definition in protocol-lock.md §4 says "TP = AFIB records predicted as AFIB, FP = non-AFIB records predicted as AFIB." This means PPV is computed only over records the model predicts as AFIB -- not over all 80% of records retained. But the retention criterion (sort by max-confidence, keep top 80%) retains high-confidence not-AFIB predictions too, and those records do not contribute to TP or FP.

This creates two distinct interpretations:

Interpretation A (stated): PPV = precision of AFIB predictions among all records the model predicts on (i.e., the 80% it does not abstain on). This is PPV on the predicted-positive subset only, not across the retained set.

Interpretation B (implied by "PPV at coverage=0.80"): PPV across all 80% retained records where coverage is the fraction of all records with a prediction (AFIB or not).

Under interpretation A, abstention helps PPV mechanically: removing low-confidence records removes the true borderline positives and false positives together, and the effect on PPV depends on which class is more common among abstained records. Under interpretation B, abstention at 80% coverage simply raises the bar for what the model acts on, and PPV is across the retained AFIB-predicted subset.

Neither interpretation is wrong, but they are not equivalent, and the protocol switches between them in different sections. approach.md §Primary metric says "among the records the model chooses to predict as AFIB ... what fraction are true AFIB" (interpretation A, AFIB-predicted only). protocol-lock.md §4 says "Sort records by max(P_afib, 1 - P_afib) descending, take the top 80%, among those PPV = TP / (TP + FP)" which is also interpretation A but the abstention criterion (sort by max-confidence) is not the same as thresholding on P_afib -- it retains high-confidence not-AFIB predictions in the 80%, which do not count toward PPV.

The net ambiguity: if a record has P_afib = 0.05 (very confident not-AFIB), it scores max(0.05, 0.95) = 0.95 and is retained in the top 80%. It is predicted not-AFIB, so it contributes neither TP nor FP. Abstractly this is fine, but it means the denominator for coverage (80% of all records) and the denominator for PPV (only predicted-AFIB records in the 80%) are different, and the relationship between coverage and PPV is not as straightforward as the clinical BASEL anchor implies.

Fix required: explicitly state that PPV(coverage=0.80) means: among records whose P_afib score exceeds the threshold tau* that retains exactly 80% of records, what fraction of AFIB-predicted records are true AFIB. Make clear that the abstention mechanism is on all-class confidence (max(P_afib, 1-P_afib)) and that PPV is computed over the AFIB-predicted subset of the retained records. Add a note in approach.md that coverage = fraction of all records retained (not fraction predicted AFIB), and that the AFIB prevalence in the retained set will differ from the full-test prevalence.

Where: approach.md §Evaluation protocol §Primary metric; protocol-lock.md §4.
What to do: add two sentences clarifying coverage denominator vs PPV denominator, and add a sentence noting that the retained subset may have a different AFIB prevalence than strat_fold 10 overall.

---

**M-2 -- Pilot P-4 risks contaminating the calibration partition (pilots-README.md §P-4)**

P-4 instructs: (1) train xresnet1d50 for 3 epochs on strat_fold 1 only, then (2) apply temperature scaling on strat_fold 9. Temperature scaling fits the T parameter on strat_fold 9. Strat_fold 9 is the designated validation / calibration partition, reserved for final temperature scaling in the headline run.

The risk: if the T value fitted in P-4 (or the Brier score / ECE values from P-4) is used to make a design decision -- even informally -- before the protocol is locked, that design decision indirectly depends on strat_fold 9. The protocol-lock correctly says strat_fold 9 is "used freely for pilots, ablations, and model selection," so this is not technically a violation. However, the protocol-lock §2 says "The seed with the lowest strat_fold 9 Brier score is selected" for the headline model -- meaning strat_fold 9 is used for seed selection. If P-4 also reveals model behavior on strat_fold 9, the seed-selection procedure in the headline is no longer based on a clean first look at that partition.

This is different from touching the held-out test partition (strat_fold 10), and the protocol makes no claim of strat_fold 9 cleanness. But there is a subtle issue: P-4 uses a 3-epoch model that is not the headline model, and it runs temperature scaling on strat_fold 9. If P-4's calibration results feed any design decisions (e.g., whether temperature scaling is adequate or whether Platt scaling is needed), those decisions are strat_fold 9-informed before the headline calibration step. This is fine in principle but the protocol does not acknowledge it, creating a documentation gap. If the final T value from the headline run is compared against P-4's T, a reader might ask whether the calibration step was tuned on the validation set.

Fix required: explicitly document in pilots-README.md P-4 and in protocol-lock.md §2 that strat_fold 9 is used for calibration in both P-4 and the headline run, and that this is intentional design (strat_fold 9 is the calibration partition, not a clean held-out). This removes the ambiguity. Do not change the partition assignment -- just add the clarifying sentence.

Where: pilots-README.md §P-4; protocol-lock.md §2.
What to do: one-sentence addition in each location: "Strat_fold 9 is used for calibration in both pilots and the headline run; this is intended design, not a protocol violation."

---

### Minor

**m-1 -- Cross-reference to ``cross-subject-eeg/approach.md §Promote`` is a forward reference to a non-existent file (approach.md §Promote on completion, Note on partition.py)**

approach.md §Promote lists partition.py and adds a note: "cross-subject-eeg is also building a subject_disjoint_split with compatible interface (see cross-subject-eeg/approach.md §Promote)." As of 2026-05-02 the worktree contains no ``20-plan/cross-subject-eeg/approach.md``. The file does not exist. The note establishes an inter-track interface assumption based on a non-existent artifact.

This is not a blocker -- the promotion note is aspirational -- but it is a source of spec drift if cross-subject-eeg's methodology takes a different approach to partition splitting.

Fix required: replace the forward reference with a note that reads "when cross-subject-eeg reaches layer 20, the two partition utilities should be compared and merged or aliased -- see the reuse-sketch in 20-plan/shared/reuse-sketches/ if present."

Where: approach.md, §Promote on completion, partition.py note.
What to do: remove or qualify the forward reference to a non-existent file.

---

**m-2 -- Headline-null definition has a gap for the "statistically significant, effect < 5 pp, CI overlaps 5 pp" case (protocol-lock.md §8)**

protocol-lock.md §8 defines:
- Headline-positive: p < 0.05 AND effect >= 5 pp (CI lower bound >= 5 pp).
- Headline-null: p >= 0.05, OR effect < 5 pp AND CI upper bound < 10 pp.
- Partial positive: p < 0.05 but effect < 5 pp.

The case p < 0.05, effect < 5 pp, CI lower bound >= 0, CI upper bound >= 10 pp falls into "partial positive" but the description says "let the reader judge." This is fine, but the case p >= 0.05, effect < 5 pp, CI upper bound >= 10 pp is not assigned to any category: it is not headline-null (CI upper bound >= 10 pp means the null cannot rule out a large effect) and not partial positive (p >= 0.05). It is a wide-CI inconclusive, and it should be named.

Fix required: add a sentence for the inconclusive case: "If p >= 0.05 and CI upper bound >= 10 pp, the result is inconclusive -- the test is underpowered for this effect size at the realized N, and findings.md must note this explicitly."

Where: protocol-lock.md §8.
What to do: one sentence added to headline-null definition covering the wide-CI inconclusive case.

---

**m-3 -- Spec line in pilots-README.md points to approach.md, not to admission.md (pilots-README.md line 1)**

pilots-README.md starts with ``> **Spec:** ``20-plan/ecg-ppg-realworld/approach.md````. The CLAUDE.md per-file table specifies that pilots-README.md spec is ``<slug>/approach.md``, so this is correct per the spec-convention table. No change needed. Noted here to confirm the check was performed.

---

**m-4 -- Human checkpoint box not ticked in admission.md (admission.md §Human checkpoint)**

admission.md shows:
  - [x] Lead reviewed critic verdict and evidence -- 2026-05-02
  - [ ] Human approval to instantiate ``20-plan/ecg-ppg-realworld/``

The second checkbox is unchecked, yet ``20-plan/ecg-ppg-realworld/`` has been instantiated and all four methodology artifacts exist. The artifacts are ahead of the checkpoint that gates instantiation. This is an operational discipline issue: either the human approved and the box was not checked, or the directory was instantiated without approval.

Fix required: confirm human approval and check the box, or document why this was waived.

Where: ``10-pain-point/ecg-ppg-realworld/admission.md`` §Human checkpoint.
What to do: check the box with date, or add a note explaining the waiver.

---

### What passes cleanly (no action needed)

- **Spec drift:** None. The four artifacts answer the admission record exactly. Scope is PTB-XL AFib abstention. Skin-tone PPG scope is explicitly dropped with the correct rationale and correct citation to the advisory. No scope creep.

- **Novelty claim:** Correctly distinguished. The claim (PPV-at-fixed-alert-rate calibrated to BASEL inconclusive rate, patient-disjoint split) is not present in Smole 2023, NCA 2024, or Barandas 2024, which are cited accurately. The claim does not over-reach. Standard components (xresnet1d50, temperature scaling, bootstrap CI) are correctly labeled as intentionally standard.

- **Pre-registration completeness:** Held-out partition (strat_fold 10, PTB-XL v1.0.3) named; primary metric (PPV at coverage=0.80) named; statistical test (paired bootstrap, n=2000, seed=42) named; H0 / H1 stated; headline-positive vs headline-null decision rule stated with numeric thresholds (5 pp effect, 10 pp CI upper bound). Pre-run checklist complete. This is the most complete pre-registration in the portfolio.

- **Compute envelope coherence:** The methodology fits GTX 1650 4 GB with margin. xresnet1d50 ~1.4 M parameters, batch=32, float32 estimated ~0.8 GB VRAM. Classical baseline is CPU-only. No Kaggle fallback claimed for this track; compute.md is not modified. The estimate is plausible for this model family on PTB-XL (confirmed by Strodthoff et al. 2020 using identical architecture). No step requires compute above local envelope.

- **Kill criteria:** All risks have numeric or yes/no triggers. R-1 (>5% records unreadable), R-2 (< 87 AFIB records combined at threshold 75.0), R-3 (both neural and classical confidence-correctness AUC < 0.55), R-5 (AUROC < 0.60 on strat_fold 9 for both models), R-7 (model selection depended on strat_fold 10). These are concrete. R-4 and R-9 explicitly have no kill criterion with documented rationale.

- **Ablations on load-bearing choices:** Five ablations covering abstention vs none, calibration vs no calibration, MC-Dropout vs temperature scaling, classical vs deep, and AFIB likelihood threshold sensitivity.
